transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
I have a sore throat.
When did your sore throat start?
2 days ago.
Any fever, chills, or difficulty swallowing?
I have fever and chills, but no difficulty swallowing.
Any cough, runny nose, or nasal congestion?
No.
Any swollen glands or rash?
I have swollen glands, but no rash.
Any history of recent exposure to someone with strep throat or COVID-19?
Yes. My son had a strep throat last week.
Have you taken any medications or treatments for your sore throat so far?
I just took tea with honey and that's about it. Nothing.
Any difficulty breathing or chest pain?
No medication.
Any difficulty opening your mouth or drooling?
No.
How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
Penicillin.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

It sounds like you have symptoms consistent with a bacterial throat infection, likely strep throat, especially given your recent exposure and fever. Because this is a phone visit, I can’t examine you or do tests, but based on what you told me, medication treatment may be appropriate. A licensed physician will review this note and, if appropriate, may prescribe an antibiotic that’s safe for you given your penicillin allergy. For now, you can continue supportive care like drinking warm fluids and using acetaminophen or ibuprofen for pain and fever if you can take them safely. 

If you develop any trouble breathing, severe throat pain, difficulty swallowing, drooling, or if your symptoms worsen significantly, please seek in-person care urgently. If you notice signs of airway blockage or severe illness, go to the emergency room right away. A clinician will review your case soon and decide on the best treatment plan.
